persons.arguments_against: Strongest case against: there are 6 Musson biographies and 5 Mussons in the staff lists, 'clerk' is the most generic of junior titles, and a son named for his father (another Samuel P. Musson) could have followed him into the Jamaica civil service. This fails: the staff list is not matching on a single generic title but on a 23-year career whose every transition matches the biography — 'Clerks, 1st Class' in the Colonial Secretary's Office in 1877-1883 (bio: '1st class clk. in col. sec.'s office, Oct., 1872'), promotion to 'Chief Clerk' appearing in the 1886 edition (bio: 'ch. clk., 1884'), and 'Public Treasurer' / 'Island Treasurer' from 1898 (bio: 'treas., Jamaica, 1896'), with the Legislative Council row matching the bio's 1897 nomination as member of the legislative council. A father/son pair cannot occupy this single continuous promotion ladder in one office; the staff lists show one S. P. Musson, not two. The stint name 'Samuel P' agrees with the full printed forename, not just initials. Five edition-years co-occur with position agreement.

evidence_cited.fact: 1st class clk. in col. sec.'s office, Oct., 1872

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of two S. P. Mussons in the Jamaica Colonial Secretary's Office or Treasury in 1877-1900 (e.g. records distinguishing a senior and junior Samuel P. Musson, or a different forename behind the Treasury rows).

notes: The honour check fails only because neither side prints an honour. The bio's promotion dates slightly precede their first staff-list appearance (chief clerk 1884 vs first listed 1886; treasurer 1896 vs first listed 1898), consistent with edition lag. The 1888 bio version's '1867' clerk date is an OCR variant of 1866.
